home.headline: How to Use Meal Order to Reduce Blood Sugar Spikes
home.subtitle: Same food, healthier life.
paragraphs: This lesson explains how food order affects glucose levels, why that matters, and one practical habit:
Start with vegetables/fiber, then protein, and save starchier carbs for last.

lessons.title: Why Care About This Topic?
paragraphs: Why care about blood sugar spikes?
lessons.callout: Goal: keep glucose stable, not spiking.

lessons.title: Track and Interpret Blood Sugar
paragraphs: Track blood glucose over time to understand what affects it.
Interpret patterns, not just one isolated number.

bullet_sections.heading: How to track
bullets: BGM (blood glucose monitor): finger-prick, single reading at one moment
CGM (continuous glucose monitor): wearable sensor, tracks glucose continuously (24/7)

bullet_sections.heading: How to interpret
bullets: If levels go outside range, review food/timing/meal-order patterns

lessons.callout: Key idea: use trends to guide your next meal decisions.

lessons.title: What Influences Blood Glucose (Up and Down)
paragraphs: Different daily factors can raise or lower glucose.

bullet_sections.heading: Can raise glucose
bullets: Not taking enough insulin or diabetes medications
Stress, illness, pain, and menstrual cycle changes

bullet_sections.heading: Can lower glucose
bullets: Too little food or missed meals/snacks
Alcohol, especially on an empty stomach
Some medication effects and cycle-related fluctuations

lessons.title: Why Meal Order Matters
paragraphs: Some studies suggest that the order you eat foods can significantly affect blood sugar spikes, even if the total meal stays the same.
In one small study of people with type 2 diabetes, participants ate the same meal in different orders (carbs first vs carbs last).
Eating protein and vegetables before carbohydrates led to lower post-meal glucose levels.
lessons.callout: The food stayed the same. The order changed.

lessons.title: Use the Diabetes Plate as the Meal Template
paragraphs: The Diabetes Plate is a practical way to build meals that support glucose management.
lessons.callout: Half veggies, quarter protein, quarter carbs.

lessons.title: Core Meal-Order Habit (Including Mixed Meals)
paragraphs: One simple way to reduce blood sugar spikes is to change the order you eat your food.
Even in mixed meals, you can still start with vegetable/protein-heavy bites first.

bullet_sections.heading: 3-step eating order
bullets: Step 1: Start with non-starchy vegetables or fiber-rich foods.
Step 3: Save starchier carbohydrates for last.

bullet_sections.heading: If the meal is mixed together
bullets: Start with bites that have more vegetables and protein first.
Delay starch-heavier bites until later when possible.
Eat more slowly to avoid a rapid carb load at the start.

lessons.callout: Default fallback: avoid carb-first eating when possible.

lessons.title: Quiz Intro
paragraphs: Answer a short series of questions to check your understanding of the material.

quiz_questions.question: Practice on one meal: which order should be eaten?
quiz_questions.reason: Best answer: salad -> chicken -> rice. This order is linked to the lower spike pattern in the prototype chart.

quiz_questions.question: Which meal order is more likely to reduce the blood sugar spike?
quiz_questions.hint: Option A: Bread first, turkey second, side salad last. Option B: Side salad first, turkey second, bread last.

choices.label: A: Bread first -> turkey -> side salad
B: Side salad first -> turkey -> bread last

quiz_questions.reason: Correct answer: B. Bread still belongs in the meal, but delaying it until after fiber and protein usually creates a gentler spike.
quiz_questions.feedback_extra: Carbohydrates are still part of the meal. The lower-spike strategy is about sequence: fiber/vegetables first, protein next, and bread/starch later.

quiz_questions.question: Breakfast version: which order is more likely to reduce the spike?
quiz_questions.hint: Option A: Toast first, eggs second, berries last. Option B: Berries first, eggs second, toast last.

choices.label: B: Berries first -> eggs -> toast last

quiz_questions.reason: Correct answer: B. It follows the same logic: fiber/produce first, protein next, starch last.
quiz_questions.feedback_extra: This does not make a sweet breakfast "free." It points to a gentler order for the same foods.

wrap_up.title: Wrap-up
wrap_up.takeaway: If the goal is to reduce the size of a post-meal glucose spike, the safer default is usually: vegetables/fiber first, protein second, starchier carbs last.
summary_points: You do not need perfect meals to make progress.
On busy days, avoid starting with starch if possible.
Repeat this order consistently to reduce average spike size over time.
